text:
Hispanics in the United States have the ability to speak more than one language. They live in a predominantly English-speaking country where many non-English languages are spoken as a second language. Researchers like the US Census Bureau and American Community Survey study how language proficiency and use in the Hispanic population is changing over time. The researchers use the data from 1980 to 2013 to find some conclusions. First, the researchers discover that the percentage of people in the Latino population ages 5 and older who can speak English proficiently increased to 68% in 2013 from 59% in 2000. The percentage of people in the Latino population who are born in the United States has been increasing while the percentage of people who are born in another country is decreasing. Currently, US-born Latinos outnumber foreign-born Latinos by a two-to-one margin. This also is a factor in how they use the languages they speak.

The record 35.8 million Hispanic homes speaking Spanish at home reflects not so much an increase in the percentage of Hispanic homes speaking Spanish at home – an increase that occurred primarily in the 1990s – but rather an increase in the size of the Hispanic population overall. The study examines the varying levels of English proficiency by Hispanic nativity, age of entry, and educational level, and reveals that the percentage of English proficient or speaking only English among the foreign-born adults has remained stable, while the percentage of the foreign-born children speaking English proficiently or exclusively has significantly increased. This shift in language proficiency reflects the broader structural change in the Hispanic population in the United States – a phenomenon that can be described as a “settle-in” effect given the fact that the U.S. born population has surpassed immigration as the principal source of Hispanic population growth.
